coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous identification of three types of tetracycline homologs via a binary-fluorescent sensor array based on nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaopei
Yu, Hang
Kuang, Fenliang
Fang, Shuju
Luo, Lijun
He, Yanzhi
Ma, Jie
Yang, Wenrong
Tan, Wei
Tan, Xuewen
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent sensor arrays can efficiently identify complex mixtures with high accuracy and rapid speed at a low cost. In this study, citric acid and urea were chosen as the carbon source and the doped nitrogen source. 5 kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCQDs) were prepared via a microwave-assisted heating method by regulating the ratio of the urea. The prepared NCQDs had selective recognition and differential response to 3 kinds of tetracycline homologs (TCs). Based on this special phenomenon, a fluorescent sensor array was constructed by using the obtained NCQDs as sensor array units for identifying tetracycline, chlorotetracycline hydrochloride, and oxytetracycline. Then the five-dimensional sensor array was simplified to the binary-dimensional sensor array using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), which had no impact on the performance for identification and classification of TCs. The pattern recognition and quantitative relationships were established by using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). The obtained LDA scatter plot clearly distinguished TCs. Additionally, a good linear relationship was established between the concentration of TCs and Factor 1. Specifically, the linear ranges of TC, CTC, and OTC were 0.5–100 μM, 9–100 μM, and 1–100 μM, respectively. Moreover, the sensor array enabled the qualitative detection of unknown TCs in water samples. This study provides a simple yet powerful fingerprint-sensing platform for TCs, offering new directions for the detection of homologs or structural analogs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor array
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Tetracycline homologs
Linear discriminant analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of NCQD2 (a) and NCQD4 (b).
TEM images of NCQD2 (a) and NCQD4 (b), XRD patterns of two types of NCQDs (c), XPS spectrum of NCQD2 (d) and NCQD4 (e), FT-IR spectrum (f).

Fingerprints of TCs generated by the raw sensor array (a) and simplified sensor array (b), Data of principal component analysis (c), Canonical score plot of the simplified sensor array for the discrimination of TCs (d).
Canonical score plots obtained from LDA for TC (a), CTC (b), and OTC (c) at different concentrations, Plots of Factor 1 versus the concentrations of TC (d), CTC (e), and OTC (f).
Canonical score plot for the discrimination of TCs in Dianchi Lake (a)-(c), and in Laoyu River (d)-(f).
UV–vis absorption spectra of TCs, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of NCQDs (a)-(b), Fluorescence decay curves of NCQDs before and after adding 50 μM TCs (c)-(d).
Schematic of the illustration of the sensor array (A), and mechanism of fluorescence quenching (B).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: ‘CESE2024-ICARE’ published in Separation and Purification Technology.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous identification of three types of tetracycline homologs via a binary-fluorescent sensor array based on nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Chemistry and Biotechnology, School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria 3216, Australia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaopei Li and Hang Yu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent sensor arrays can efficiently identify complex mixtures with high accuracy and rapid speed at a low cost. In this study, citric acid and urea were chosen as the carbon source and the doped nitrogen source. 5 kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCQDs) were prepared via a microwave-assisted heating method by regulating the ratio of the urea. The prepared NCQDs had selective recognition and differential response to 3 kinds of tetracycline homologs (TCs). Based on this special phenomenon, a fluorescent sensor array was constructed by using the obtained NCQDs as sensor array units for identifying tetracycline, chlorotetracycline hydrochloride, and oxytetracycline. Then the five-dimensional sensor array was simplified to the binary-dimensional sensor array using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), which had no impact on the performance for identification and classification of TCs. The pattern recognition and quantitative relationships were established by using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). The obtained LDA scatter plot clearly distinguished TCs. Additionally, a good linear relationship was established between the concentration of TCs and Factor 1. Specifically, the linear ranges of TC, CTC, and OTC were 0.5–100 μM, 9–100 μM, and 1–100 μM, respectively. Moreover, the sensor array enabled the qualitative detection of unknown TCs in water samples. This study provides a simple yet powerful fingerprint-sensing platform for TCs, offering new directions for the detection of homologs or structural analogs.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline homologs (TCs) have been widely applied in human therapy and agricultural production owing to their broad-spectrum antibacterial activity [1]. However, TCs are not fully absorbed by the human body, around 5–90 % of the TCs are excreted into the environment via urine and feces. This ultimately pollutes the surface water and groundwater resources [2]. Therefore, it is necessary to simultaneously identify and quantify different TCs in the aqueous environment with fast, sensitive, and low-cost. To date, multitudinous analytical methods have been well-established for the TCs detection, including high-performance liquid chromatography [3], capillary electrophoresis [4], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5], and so on. Most of these methods are confronted with problems such as sophisticated and costly instruments, complicated pretreatment processes, time-consuming, and not suitable for on-site detection [6]. By contrast, the fluorescent probes technique has significant advantages in sensitivity, selectivity, cost-effectiveness, and efficiency, which have received widespread attention in recent years [7–11].

Up to now, various fluorescent probes have been explored to sensitively detect TCs [12,13]. However, these detection methods are based on the “lock-key” pattern of the material to the target. The specific recognition is usually affected by concurrent substances, particularly by homologs or structural analogs. It is liable to produce false-positive signals, resulting in distorted test results [14,15]. Furthermore, it does not classify and recognize complex mixtures, which severely limits the accurate detection of tetracycline homologs. Hence, there is a formidable technical challenge in the concurrent classification and detection of multiple structural analogs by using fluorescence technology.

Sensor arrays with cross-reactive properties are a novel detection strategy inspired by the biological senses of smell and taste. It is known as the electronic nose and tongue and is becoming a hotspot in the sensor field [16,17]. The sensor arrays consist of a plurality of sensing units that generate distinct response signals to analytes. The specific fingerprints of different analytes can be obtained through cross-reactive response signals of units. Then multiple analytes can be discriminated and detected by using data analysis and model training to recognize the fingerprints. The sensor arrays not only detect multiple analytes simultaneously but also recognize multiple targets in complex mixtures, which is more suitable for detecting targeted analytes in complex matrix samples or identifying structural analogs [18]. Currently, many sensor arrays have been exploited to identify heavy metal ions [19], pesticides [16], and bacteria [20]. However, as far as we know, there is rarely reported literature on fluorescent sensor arrays constructed with nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots for tetracycline homologs detection.

The fluorescent sensor arrays can recognize a variety of analytes, exhibiting high capacity and anti-interference. However, the recognition performance also relies on the material properties of the individual sensing units. Some new materials are being used as sensing units, including organic fluorescent dyes [21], biomolecules [22], polymers [23], and nanomaterials [24]. In recent years, the development of nanomaterials has provided more options for the design of sensor arrays. Carbon nanodots, as a type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have been extensively investigated [25]. Carbon nanodots can be synthesized by the facile method of microwave-assisted or solvothermal. It is capable of emitting a stable fluorescent light, which has broad application prospects in chemical sensing [26] and biosensing [27]. To further explore the versatile properties of carbon nanodots, nitrogen doping is often used to alter the electronic structure of carbon nanodots to enhance their fluorescent properties and chemical stability [28]. Further exploration of high-performance nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots is beneficial for the construction of sensor arrays.

Herein, a fluorescence sensor array with multiple channels was constructed for tetracycline homologs detection by using 5 kinds of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCQDs) (Scheme 1 ). The NCQDs were prepared by a microwave-assisted heating method using citric acid and urea as raw materials. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) were used to simplify the sensor array and develop the identification scheme, respectively. 3 kinds of tetracycline homologs were precisely identified. The performance of recognition and anti-interference was optimized and then evaluated by using real water samples. The constructed fluorescent sensor array demonstrated excellent performance in pattern recognition.

Citrazinic acid (C6H8O7·H2O) and Urea (CO(NH2)2) were obtained from Tianjin Bodi Chemicals Co. and Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., respectively. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and di-potassium hydrogen phosphate trihydrate (K2HPO4·3H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), Chlorotetracycline hydrochloride (C22H23ClN2O8·HCl), and Oxytetracycline (C22H24N2O9) were attained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The purity of all reagents was illustrated in Table S1. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water (18.2 Ω).

TEM (Japan, JEM-F200) was used to determine the morphology and size distribution of NCQDs. Elemental composition was analyzed using XPS (USA, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). A steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (UK, Edinburgh FLS1000) was applied to detect fluorescence lifetime. FT-IR (USA, Nicolet) was utilized to determine the characteristic surface groups of the NCQDs. Fluorescence spectra were measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan, F-7000).

NCQDs were synthesized by a one-step microwave method using citric acid and urea as raw materials. Briefly, 1 g of citrazinic acid and 3 g of urea were mixed and dissolved in 17.5 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixture was transferred into a household microwave oven and reacted at 360 W for 7 min. The resulting solid product was fully dissolved in ultrapure water and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO:500) for 24 h to remove the excessive precursors and small molecules. After freeze drying, the NCQD1 was obtained and maintained at 4℃ for further use. Other NCQDs (NCQD2, NCQD3, NCQD4, and NCQD5) were prepared using the same procedure, except with different mass ratios of citric acid and urea (1:2, 1:1, 2:1, and 3:1).

Different concentrations of TCs (24 μL) were mixed with different NCQDs solutions (500 μL, 0.4 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 8.0, 2476 μL). Then, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture at specific excitation wavelengths was determined and recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer. The response signals of individual NCQDs to individual TCs can be obtained and normalized using (F0-F)/F0 (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs in the presence and absence of analytes, respectively). Raw data was firstly analyzed by PCA using SPSS software to reduce the dimensionality of the sensor array. After that, the new data of the simplified array was imported into R Studio for LDA analysis. The sensing performance of NCQDs for TCs was analyzed using fluorescence response patterns mapped in R Studio. All of the TCs were recognized and quantitatively detected by using the new sensor array.

NCQD2 and NCQD4 used in the sensor array were characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. 1 (a), the UV–vis absorption peaks of NCQD2 at 269 nm and 331 nm can be attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively, which are mainly caused by the CC and CO bonds [29]. The wavelengths of the maximum excitation and emission are located at 402 nm and 528 nm, respectively. The NCQD4 has two UV–vis absorption peaks at 209 nm and 340 nm, which also can be ascribed to the transitions of π-π* (CC) and n-π* (CO) [30], respectively (Fig. 1 (b)). The maximum emission wavelength is 469 nm upon excitation at 373 nm.

The morphology and size distribution of NCQD2 and NCQD4 were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the prepared NCQD2 and NCQD4 are uniformly distributed and have a spherical structure with good monodispersity. The average particle sizes of NCQD2 and NCQD4 are 6.298 ± 0.094 nm and 2.074 ± 0.022 nm, respectively. The HRTEM image shows that NCQD2 has a significant lattice crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, which is the contribution of the 002 plane of graphitic carbon nitride [31]. NCQD4 has a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [32]. Then, their crystal structure was performed by using XRD and exhibited in Fig. 2 (c). The peaks of NCQD2 located at 27.08⁰ and 13.74⁰ can be attributed to the (002) plane of the typical inter-planar stacking of aromatic systems and the (100) plane of the tris-s-triheptazine unit in the lamellar structure [33], respectively. The peak of NCQD4 located at 26.98⁰ corresponds to the graphite-like phase diffraction peak (002) with an interlayer distance of 0.34 nm [34]. It is evident that the crystallinity of NCQDs has been adjusted by adding different mass ratios of urea. Combined with TEM results, it demonstrates that the NCQD2 are graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots, and the NCQD4 are graphene quantum dots. This is the main reason for their different optical properties [35].

The FT-IR spectra of NCQD2 and NCQD4 are presented in Fig. 2 (f). It can be seen that both of them have a similar absorption feature. The characteristic absorption bands around 3442 cm−1 and 3164 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively. The pronounced absorption peaks at 1707, 1600, 1357, and 1191 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, CN, and CN, respectively. The above results indicate that oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups exist on the surface of the NCQD2 and NCQD4 [36].

XPS was further utilized to determine the chemical composition and surface groups of NCQDs. The full-scan XPS spectra demonstrate that both NCQD2 (Fig. 2 (d)) and NCQD4 (Fig. 2 (e)) contained C, N, and O elements. However, the nitrogen content of NCQD2 was significantly enhanced by about 5.64 % due to the increase of nitrogen doping. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s are presented in Fig. S2, and the detailed analysis is shown in Table S2. It is worth noting that the N 1 s spectrum of NCQD2 can be fitted into three peaks of CNH (398.62 eV), CNC (399.71 eV), and N (C)3 (400.42 eV), respectively. This indicates that the NCQD2 is the graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots [31,34,37]. Whereas, three peaks fitted in the N 1 s spectrum of NCQD4 are assigned to Pyridinic N (398.83 eV), Pyrrolic N (400.00 eV), and Graphitic N (400.81 eV), respectively. Thus, the NCQD4 can be seen as nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [38,39].

Firstly, tetracycline (TC), chlorotetracycline (CTC), and oxytetracycline (OTC) effectively quenched the fluorescence of NCQDs, but the response signals ((F0-F)/F0) varied owing to the different quenched degree (Fig. S3). Based on this, the characteristic fingerprint data of TC, CTC, and OTC were obtained and shown in Fig. 3 (a). In order to simplify the complexity, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was chosen to statistically analyze the five-dimensional fingerprint data because it is a well-known method for dimensional reduction for sensor arrays [40,41]. As illustrated in Fig. 3 (c), the variance contribution rates of the first principal component (PC1) and second principal component (PC2) were obtained as 72.34 % and 18.81 %, respectively, and the combined contribution rates achieved 91.15 %. This indicates that PC1 and PC2 could be chosen as principal components to construct the sensor array. Additionally, the NCQD4 had the highest contribution to PC1 with a correlation coefficient of 92.20 %, while the main contribution to PC2 was NCQD2 with a correlation coefficient of 89.50 %, indicating that NCQD2 and NCQD4 played a primary role in the array. Therefore, NCQD2 and NCQD4 were selected as the units to construct the new sensor array, and the fingerprints are exhibited in Fig. 3 (b). The training matrix of the simplified sensor array was 2 sensing units × 3 target pollutants × 5 replicates. The generated fluorescent response patterns were then subjected to Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and the canonical score plot was demonstrated in Fig. 3(d). Each TCs at 50 μM had its own distribution area with good spatial separation. The identification accuracy achieved 100 % (Factor 1 = 74.87 %, Factor 2 = 25.13 %). This indicates that the simplified fluorescent sensor array had effective recognition and differentiation of TCs based on NCQD2 and NCQD4.

The pH value is the most important factor in fluorescent sensing [42,43]. So the response performance of two sensor units to TCs was evaluated at various pH values (1–14). The pH-related fingerprint maps and corresponding LDA scatter plots were shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8. The performance of the sensor array was affected when the pH was lower than 4 and greater than 8. This can be attributed to the protonation effect of the NCQDs under a strong acid or alkali environment [44]. Better response performance could be acquired in LDA scatter plots in a wide pH range (4–8). Given the potential application in environmental pollutant identification, the pH value of 8 was selected for subsequent research [45]. In addition, we considered the effects of salinity and temperature on the performance of the sensor array. As the concentration of NaCl increases from 0 to 1 M, the minimal fluctuation in the fluorescence intensity of the sensor array was observed (Figs. S9-S12). This observation suggests that our sensor array demonstrates a favorable tolerance to salinity. With the gradual increase in temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor array displays varying degrees of fluctuation. (Figs. S13-S16). The findings suggest that the sensor array is well-suited for detection within room temperature ranges.

Different concentrations (0.1–500 μM) of TCs were chosen to further evaluate the performance of quantitative detection. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , TC could be efficiently distinguished, and a good linear relationship was achieved between Factor 1 (97.44 %) and the concentration of TC in the range of 0.5–100 μM. Two linear regression equations (Factor 1 = 9.463 CTC-249.3 and Factor 1 = 3.321 CTC −132.7) were calculated with correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9976 and 0.9927 (Fig. 4 (a) and (d)). For CTC, the linear relationship between Factor 1 (99.74 %) and the concentration was acquired in the range of 9–100 μM. The linear equation can be exhibited as Factor 1 = 1.704 CCTC −105.4 with R2 of 0.9943 (Fig. 4 (b) and (e)). When the concentration of OTC changed from 1 to 100 μM, a good linear relationship can be obtained and the linear equation was shown as Factor 1 (99.64 %) = 1.085 COTC-58.77 with R2 of 0.9919 (Fig. 4 (c) and (f)). In addition, the detection limit of TC, CTC, and OTC were calculated as 0.4612 μM, 2.5612 μM, and 4.0223 μM, respectively, according to the formula 3 N/k. In this context, N represents the standard deviation of the blank (n = 20), while k denotes the slope of the calibration curve. These results suggested that the fluorescent sensor array could simultaneously and quantitatively detect these three tetracycline homologs at the concentration range of 9–100 μM.

Firstly, two water samples obtained from Dianchi Lake (Kunming, China) and Laoyu River (Kunming, China) were chosen to examine the recognition performance of the sensor array by using the standard addition method with the addition concentration of 20, 50, and 80 μM. The physicochemical properties (pH, temperature, salinity, and turbidity) of each actual water sample were listed in Table S4. All samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane before being used. The LDA score plot (Fig. 5 ) revealed that each of the TCs was clustered with its own distribution area. There was no cross-overlap at the above three spiked concentrations. The result indicates that the sensor array has excellent anti-interference capability, practicality, and stability for the discrimination of TCs with an accuracy of 100 % in real water samples.

Secondly, to evaluate the identification ability, double-bind experiments were conducted. Two unknown TCs (denoted as M5 and M6) were randomly selected and added to the water samples. The corresponding data was analyzed and plotted in the LDA scatter plot (Fig. S17). The data points of M5 are close to the data points of TC, which means that M5 may be the TC. Similarly, M6 can be judged as CTC [45]. These results demonstrated that the constructed sensor array had great potential applicability in real water sample analysis.

To understand the recognition mechanism of the used NCQDs to TCs. The optical properties were first investigated and shown in Fig. 6 (a)-(b). It clearly displays that the characteristic UV–vis absorption band of TC, CTC, and OTC overlap with the excitation spectra of the two NCQDs, indicating the internal filtering effect (IFE) occurred in the system [46,47]. However, the two NCQDs have different spectral overlap degrees, indicating that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of NCQD4 was more effective than that of NCQD2 via IFE [48]. This is attributed to the different core–shell structures of the NCQDs, which could be prepared via a simple strategy by altering the ratio of urea [49,50]. No photo-generated electron transfer (PET) occurred between NCQDs and TCs, which was measured by cyclic voltammetry, and the result was shown in Fig. S19. Finally, fluorescence lifetime decays of NCQDs were measured before and after adding TCs (Fig. 6 (c)–(d)). The average fluorescence lifetime (τave) of NCQD2 is 6.78 ns. After adding TCs, the τave for TC, CTC, and OTC was detected to be 6.73 ns, 6.81 ns, and 6.43 ns, respectively. However, there is almost no change in the τave of NCQD4 (7.65 ns) after adding TC (7.61 ns), but the τave for CTC and OTC change to 6.97 ns and 7.51 ns, respectively. Obviously, TC quenched the fluorescence of NCQD2 and NCQD4 via a static quenching procedure, and OTC quenched the fluorescence of NCQD2 and NCQD4 through a dynamic process. Whereas the CTC quenched the fluorescence of NCQD2 and NCQD4 through static and dynamic mode, respectively [51,52]. This difference in fluorescence quenching mode is the foundation for constructing the fluorescent sensor array.

This work provided a simple strategy for preparing different kinds of NCQDs by adjusting the proportion of the nitrogen source. The prepared NCQDs exhibited selective recognition and differential response to three kinds of TCs because of their difference in structure, optical property, and interaction process with each other. Thus, the obtained 5 kinds of NCQDs were used to construct a raw sensor array. After principal component analysis, a binary-dimensional sensor array was constructed by using NCQD2 and NCQD4 as sensor units. The linear discriminant analysis was selected to establish the pattern recognition scheme and quantitative relationships for TCs. The simplified fluorescent sensor array can simultaneously and quantitatively detect three tetracycline homologs at the concentration range of 9–100 μM. Satisfactory results were obtained in the actual water sample analysis with an accuracy of 100 %. The good stability and high identification capability indicate that the simplified sensor array has good application prospects.

Xiaopei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hang Yu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Fenliang Kuang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Shuju Fang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lijun Luo: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yanzhi He: Methodology, Investigation. Jie Ma: Writing – review & editing. Wenrong Yang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wei Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xuewen Tan: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Yunnan Province, China (No. 202501AT070361), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 22266036), YMU-DEAKIN International Associated Laboratory on Functional Materials, Education Department of Yunnan Province [No. 117-02001001002107], and College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project of China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2025.133438.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Chem. Eng. J.
